title: Introduction to Computer Systems
summary: Overview of what computers are, their history, and how binary information is processed.
content_html:
What Is a Computer?

A computer system is a machine that processes information. Every computer performs three fundamental operations:

  • Representation - encoding data as bits (0s and 1s)
  • Manipulation - applying logic and arithmetic to change the data
  • Storage - holding data in memory (registers, RAM, disk)

Bits and Bytes

All data in a computer is stored as binary digits (bits). Groups of bits form larger units:

Bit    = one binary digit (0 or 1)
Nibble = 4 bits
Byte   = 8 bits (smallest addressable unit of memory)
Word   = register size of CPU (8, 16, 32, or 64 bits)

Shorthand: 2 hex digits = 1 byte
  e.g. 11111111 = 0xFF = 255

The computer does not know what bits mean - the same 32-bit pattern can be an integer, a float, a pointer, or four ASCII characters, depending on how the program interprets it.

Analogue vs Digital

The real world is analogue - continuous quantities like voltage, temperature, sound. Computers are digital - they represent data as discrete values.

  • Analogue: good for real-world measurements; sensitive to noise
  • Digital: easy to store, transmit, and process; immune to small signal variations

Digital systems represent bits as voltages. In TTL logic: less than 0.8V = 0, greater than 2.4V = 1. This wide gap makes digital systems robust against noise.

Why binary? Only two states needed. Shannon-Hartley laws show it is robust over long cables and noisy channels. Implementing decimal with voltages would require 10 distinguishable voltage levels - impractical.

A Brief History of Computing

  • Charles Babbage (1791-1871) - Designed mechanical computing engines
  • Claude Shannon (1937) - Showed Boolean algebra can describe switching circuits
  • Konrad Zuse (1938) - Built first programmable electromechanical computers
  • John von Neumann (1945) - Defined the stored-program architecture still used today
  • Mauchly and Eckert (1946) - Built ENIAC, one of the first electronic computers
  • Williams and Kilburn (1948) - First computer to run a stored program

Major Computing Paradigms

  • Batch processing (1950s-60s): Jobs queued on punch cards; 24-hour turnaround
  • Time-sharing (1960s-70s): Many terminals, one CPU; interactive use
  • Personal computing (1980s): Computers in every home
  • Networking/Internet (1990s-2000s): Global connectivity
  • Embedded systems: Computers inside appliances, ATMs, vehicles
  • Cloud and Edge computing (2010s-present): Compute as a utility

Moore's Law

Gordon Moore observed that the number of transistors on a chip doubles approximately every two years. This exponential growth drove massive improvements in speed, memory, and cost over decades.

What You Will Learn in COS10004

  • Read and write ARM assembly language programs
  • Identify hardware components and the functions they perform
  • Understand how compilers, assemblers, linkers, and interpreters work
  • Argue trade-offs between hardware and software in computer system design

exercises.title: Claude Shannon
exercises.prompt: What was Claude Shannon's key contribution to computer systems?
options: Showed Boolean algebra can describe electrical circuits
exercises.answer: Showed Boolean algebra can describe electrical circuits

exercises.title: Byte size
exercises.prompt: How many bits are in one byte?

exercises.title: Binary representation
exercises.prompt: Why do computers use binary (0s and 1s) rather than decimal?
options: Binary is simple, robust, and easy to implement with electronic circuits
Humans designed computers to use binary
exercises.answer: Binary is simple, robust, and easy to implement with electronic circuits

options: The register size used by the CPU
exercises.answer: The register size used by the CPU
